questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The list of items ___ on the desk."
questions.explanation: The verb must agree with the true subject, not with a noun in an intervening phrase. The subject is the singular noun "list," while "of items" is a prepositional phrase that does not change the number of the subject. A singular subject takes the singular verb "is."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Neither the manager nor the workers ___ satisfied with the plan."
questions.explanation: When subjects are joined by "neither, nor" the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. The nearer subject is the plural noun "workers," so the verb must be plural, giving "are." This is the proximity rule for correlative conjunctions.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Each of the students ___ a textbook."
questions.explanation: The word "each" is always singular and controls the verb, even when followed by "of" plus a plural noun. The subject is "each," not "students," so the singular verb "has" is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The number of applicants ___ increasing every year."
questions.explanation: The phrase "the number of" is treated as a singular subject and takes a singular verb, so "is" is correct. By contrast, "a number of" means "several" and takes a plural verb. The definite article "the" signals the singular meaning here.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Mathematics ___ my favourite subject."
questions.explanation: Some nouns end in "s" but are singular in meaning, including subjects of study such as mathematics, physics, and economics. "Mathematics" names one subject and so takes the singular verb "is."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Either the teacher or the prefects ___ going to lock the hall."
questions.explanation: With "either, or" the verb agrees with the nearer subject. The closer subject is the plural noun "prefects," so the plural verb "are" is correct. This follows the same proximity rule as "neither, nor."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "My trousers ___ in the wash."
questions.explanation: Some nouns naming a single item made of two joined parts, such as trousers, scissors, and glasses, are treated as plural and take a plural verb. "Trousers" therefore takes "are."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Bread and butter ___ a common breakfast here."
questions.explanation: When two nouns joined by "and" name a single combined idea or dish, they are treated as one unit and take a singular verb. "Bread and butter" is eaten as one thing, so the singular verb "is" is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "One of the boys ___ broken the window."
questions.explanation: In "one of the boys," the subject is the singular word "one," and "of the boys" is a prepositional phrase. The verb must agree with "one," so the singular "has" is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The jury ___ divided in their opinions."
questions.explanation: A collective noun takes a plural verb when the members are acting as separate individuals rather than as one unit. The word "divided" shows the jurors disagree among themselves, so the plural verb "are" matches this individual sense.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Everybody ___ welcome at the meeting."
questions.explanation: Indefinite pronouns such as everybody, everyone, somebody, and nobody are grammatically singular and take a singular verb. "Everybody" therefore takes "is," even though it refers to many people.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The news ___ better than we expected."
questions.explanation: The word "news" ends in "s" but is an uncountable singular noun, so it takes a singular verb. The correct form is "is." We cannot say "a news" or "two news," which confirms its singular, uncountable nature.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Ten kilometres ___ a long way to walk."
questions.explanation: When a plural amount of distance, money, or time is treated as a single total, it takes a singular verb. "Ten kilometres" is regarded here as one distance, so the singular verb "is" is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The boy who owns these goats ___ here."
questions.explanation: In a relative clause the verb agrees with the noun the relative pronoun refers back to, but here the main verb belongs to the singular subject "the boy." "The boy ... lives here" needs the singular verb "lives." The plural noun "goats" inside the relative clause does not control the main verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Both the captain and the coach ___ present at the match."
questions.explanation: Two singular subjects joined by "both, and" or by a plain "and" form a plural subject and take a plural verb. The captain and the coach together require the plural verb "were."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Each boy and each girl ___ given a prize."
questions.explanation: When subjects are preceded by "each" or "every," they are treated as singular even when joined by "and." "Each boy and each girl" is therefore singular and takes the singular verb "was."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The scissors ___ on the table."
questions.explanation: Tools made of two joined blades, such as scissors, take a plural verb in standard usage. "The scissors" therefore takes "are." To speak of one item we say "a pair of scissors," and then "pair" controls the verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "There ___ several reasons for the delay."
questions.explanation: In sentences beginning with "there," the verb agrees with the real subject that follows it. The real subject is the plural noun "reasons," so the plural verb "are" is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Physics ___ considered a difficult subject by many."
questions.explanation: Like mathematics, the name of the subject "physics" ends in "s" but is singular, so it takes a singular verb. The correct form is "is."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "My family ___ planning to travel next month."
questions.explanation: A collective noun takes a singular verb when the group is acting together as a single unit. Here the family is making one shared plan, so the singular verb "is" fits the unified sense. Of the options, only "is" agrees with this single-unit reading.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Half of the cake ___ already gone."
questions.explanation: With fractions and words like "half," the verb agrees with the noun after "of." "Cake" here is a singular, uncountable amount, so the singular verb "is" is correct. If the noun were plural, as in "half of the cakes," the verb would be plural.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "The teacher, along with her pupils, ___ attending the fair."
questions.explanation: Phrases such as "along with," "as well as," and "together with" do not join subjects the way "and" does. The subject stays the singular "teacher," so the verb is singular, giving "is." The phrase set off by commas is extra information that does not change the number of the subject.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "Two thirds of the work ___ done."
questions.explanation: With a fraction such as "two thirds," the verb agrees with the noun following "of." "Work" is a singular, uncountable noun, so the singular verb form "has been" is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correct verb form: "He is one of those people who ___ never satisfied."
questions.explanation: In the pattern "one of those who," the relative pronoun "who" refers back to the plural noun "people," so the verb in the relative clause is plural. The correct form is "are," because it is the people, not the single person, that the clause describes.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide
